reference: GATE CS — Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

map.condition: Comparing pipelined vs non-pipelined execution time for n instructions
Speedup from pipelining for large n
Amdahl's law: part of program sped up by factor f
Stalls added due to data hazard / branch penalty

formulas.name: CPU Execution Time
formulas.whenToUse: Computing total execution time given instruction count, CPI, and clock period

terms.means: duration of one clock cycle (1 / clock frequency)

formulas.trap: Mixing up CPI with IPC (instructions per cycle); also confusing clock period with frequency
formulas.reference: Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

formulas.name: Clock Period and Frequency
formulas.latex: t_{cycle} = \frac{1}{f_{clock}} \quad (\text{in nanoseconds if } f \text{ in GHz})
formulas.whenToUse: Converting between clock frequency and clock period to compute execution time
formulas.trap: Forgetting unit conversions: 1 GHz = 10^9 Hz, giving t_cycle in nanoseconds
formulas.reference: Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

formulas.name: Weighted Average CPI
formulas.plain: CPI_avg = sum over i of (fraction_i * CPI_i)
formulas.whenToUse: Multiple instruction types each with their own CPI and frequency of occurrence

terms.means: fraction of instructions of type i (must sum to 1)

formulas.trap: Using instruction count instead of fraction; or forgetting to verify fractions sum to 1
formulas.reference: Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

formulas.name: Pipelined Execution Time (n instructions, k stages)
formulas.whenToUse: Total time to complete n instructions on a k-stage pipeline with uniform stage time

terms.means: clock cycle time (= max stage latency for a balanced pipeline)

formulas.trap: Using k*n (non-pipelined) instead of (k+n-1); the n-1 term accounts for fill-up of the pipeline
formulas.reference: Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

formulas.name: Non-Pipelined Execution Time
formulas.whenToUse: Baseline time without pipelining when each instruction takes k stages each of duration t_stage
formulas.trap: Confusing this with T_pipeline; a single instruction takes k*t_stage in BOTH cases
formulas.reference: Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

formulas.name: Pipeline Cycle Time (Non-uniform Stages)
formulas.whenToUse: Stages have different latencies; the slowest stage (bottleneck) determines clock period

terms.means: inter-stage latch/register overhead (often 0 in GATE problems)

formulas.trap: Using average or sum of stage times instead of the maximum
formulas.reference: Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

formulas.name: Single-Instruction Latency (Pipelined vs Non-Pipelined)
formulas.whenToUse: Comparing latency of ONE instruction; pipelining does NOT reduce single-instruction latency

terms.means: time per stage (pipeline overhead can make it slightly worse)

formulas.trap: Assuming pipelining speeds up individual instructions — it only improves throughput over many instructions
formulas.reference: Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

formulas.name: Pipeline Speedup (n instructions)
formulas.whenToUse: Computing speedup of pipelining over sequential execution for n instructions and k stages
formulas.trap: For small n the speedup is far less than k; only as n -> infinity does S approach k
formulas.reference: Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

formulas.name: Ideal Pipeline Speedup (large n)
formulas.whenToUse: Theoretical upper bound on speedup equals the number of pipeline stages
formulas.trap: Hazards and stalls reduce actual speedup below k even for large n
formulas.reference: Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

formulas.name: Amdahl's Law
formulas.whenToUse: A fraction p of total time is sped up by factor s; compute overall speedup

terms.means: fraction of execution time that is enhanced (0 <= p <= 1)
speedup factor for the enhanced portion

formulas.trap: p is a TIME fraction, not an instruction-count fraction; confusing the two gives wrong answers
formulas.reference: Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

formulas.name: CPI-Based Speedup Between Two Processors
formulas.whenToUse: Comparing performance of two processors executing the same or different workloads

terms.means: average clock cycles per instruction for each processor
clock cycle time for each processor

formulas.trap: When the same ISA/program is used, IC cancels; do not cancel IC when processors run different code
formulas.reference: Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

formulas.name: Types of Pipeline Hazards
formulas.whenToUse: Classifying the cause of pipeline stalls to choose the correct mitigation

terms.means: Read After Write — true data dependency (most common in GATE)

formulas.trap: WAR and WAW are not true dependencies; they can be eliminated by register renaming, unlike RAW
formulas.reference: Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

formulas.name: RAW Stall Cycles
formulas.whenToUse: Computing how many stall cycles a RAW hazard inserts (without forwarding)

terms.means: stage index where the result is written back (e.g., WB = stage 5)
stage index where the dependent instruction needs the value (e.g., EX = stage 3)

formulas.trap: With full forwarding from EX/MEM/WB, stalls can be reduced to 0 for most ALU-ALU dependencies but not load-use
formulas.reference: Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

formulas.name: Load-Use Hazard (1 Stall Even with Forwarding)
formulas.plain: Load followed immediately by dependent instruction -> 1 mandatory stall cycle
formulas.whenToUse: A load instruction is immediately followed by an instruction that reads the loaded register

terms.means: sequence: LOAD Rd; USE Rd — the next instruction needs the memory value

formulas.trap: Forwarding can hide most RAW hazards but cannot hide the load-use hazard; 1 bubble is unavoidable
formulas.reference: Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

formulas.name: Branch Penalty (Control Hazard)
formulas.plain: Branch penalty = k - 1 cycles (without branch prediction / delayed branching)
formulas.whenToUse: Pipeline stalls due to a branch instruction; pipeline is flushed for k-1 stages after branch

terms.means: cycles wasted while the branch target is fetched

formulas.trap: With delayed branching (1 delay slot filled usefully), effective penalty = k - 2; read problem carefully
formulas.reference: Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

formulas.name: Effective CPI with Stalls
formulas.whenToUse: Computing realistic CPI after accounting for data and control hazard stalls

terms.means: ideal CPI (= 1 for a fully pipelined processor)
weighted average stall cycles contributed by all hazard sources

formulas.trap: Must weight stall cycles by frequency of hazard-causing instructions (e.g., branch frequency * penalty)
formulas.reference: Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

formulas.name: Data Forwarding (Bypass) Condition
formulas.latex: \text{Forward if: } \exists \text{ earlier instr. } j < i \text{ such that } Rd_j = Rs_i \text{ and result available early enough}
formulas.plain: Forward if a previous instruction's destination register matches current source register and result is ready in time
formulas.whenToUse: Determining whether a stall can be eliminated by forwarding from EX/MEM/WB stage outputs

terms.means: destination register of earlier instruction j
source register of current instruction i

formulas.trap: Data forwarding can eliminate RAW stalls for ALU-to-ALU but NOT for load-to-use (value is not ready in time)
formulas.reference: Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

formulas.name: RAW Dependency Detection Rule
formulas.latex: \text{RAW: Instr } i \text{ writes } R_x, \text{ Instr } j > i \text{ reads } R_x \text{ before } R_x \text{ is written}
formulas.plain: RAW: instruction i writes Rx, then instruction j (j > i) reads Rx before Rx is updated
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying which instruction pairs create true data dependencies in a sequence

terms.means: the register that creates the dependency
instruction indices (i < j means i executes before j)

formulas.trap: Only the most RECENT writer to R_x before the reader matters; older writes are shadowed
formulas.reference: Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

formulas.name: Pipeline Throughput
formulas.whenToUse: Computing instructions completed per unit time for a pipeline with n instructions
formulas.trap: Maximum throughput is 1/t_cycle (one instruction per cycle), achieved only as n -> infinity
formulas.reference: Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

formulas.name: Pipeline Efficiency
formulas.whenToUse: Measuring utilization of pipeline stages; ranges from 0 to 1
formulas.trap: Efficiency approaches 1 only for large n; for small n it is noticeably below 1
formulas.reference: Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design

formulas.name: Pipelining Speedup with Branch Penalty
formulas.whenToUse: Adjusting ideal speedup k downward when a fraction f_b of instructions are branches with penalty p

terms.means: fraction of instructions that are branches

formulas.trap: Penalty p is in units of cycles; multiply by f_b (not by count of branches) to get stalls per instruction
formulas.reference: Computer Organization and Architecture / Processor Design
